summary: The session focused on the influence of traditional African music on contemporary genres worldwide, highlighting notable styles such as Griot, Mbube, Taarab, Juju, and Congolese Rumba. The user expressed interest in modern African musicians and their albums, as well as the recognition of African music on a global scale.
facts: Griot music is a traditional form of music and storytelling from West Africa, performed by griots who are known as keepers of history and culture. Mbube is a style of South African music characterized by a cappella harmonies and call-and-response vocals, influencing Western music styles like Doo-wop and Gospel. Taarab music is an East African genre that emerged in the late 19th century as a fusion of Swahili and Arabic music, influencing contemporary genres like Bongo Flava. Juju music is a genre of Nigerian popular music that emerged in the 1920s, combining traditional Yoruba music with Western instruments. Congolese rumba music originated in the Democratic Republic of Congo, known for its fusion of African rhythms and Latin American music, significantly impacting salsa and other Latin dance styles. Congolese musicians like Tabu Ley Rochereau and Franco Luambo Makiadi gained international fame in the 1960s and 70s with their fusion of African rhythms and Latin American music, called "soukous." Modern African musicians influenced by traditional genres include Amadou & Mariam, Angelique Kidjo, Seun Kuti, Tinariwen, Burna Boy, Sauti Sol, and Oumou Sangaré. Up-and-coming African musicians to watch include Sho Madjozi, Nasty C, Simi, Omah Lay, Elaine, and Darkovibes. Recommended albums include "Welcome to Mali" by Amadou & Mariam, "Remain in Light" by Angelique Kidjo, "From Africa with Fury: Rise" by Seun Kuti, and "African Giant" by Burna Boy. African music has historically been underrepresented globally, but recent years have seen a growing appreciation, with artists like Burna Boy receiving Grammy nominations.
